text: The patient is positioned flat for the laparoscopic ultra low anterior resection. The omentum is moved towards the stomach, and the small bowel is seen falling away from the dissection field. The surgeon ensures the area up to the DJ flexure is free, using gauze to clean any small oozing for better visibility.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and makes a small incision on the peritoneum covering the mesocolon of the sigmoid at the sacral promontory. The incision is extended superficially, allowing carbon dioxide insufflation to open up the tissue planes. The peritoneal incision is carried towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), facilitating the removal of lymphovascular structures surrounding the IMA root. Due to the low level of anastomosis at the pelvic floor, the IMA is ligated at its root, leaving a few centimeters of the aorta to reduce tension on the preserved left colic artery.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to open avascular planes with carbon dioxide insufflation. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is clearly visible, and short bursts of cautery are applied at low wattage to minimize fumes. The dissection progresses cranially towards the take-off of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook or spatula to perform dissection with traction and counter traction, advancing beyond the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. Carbon dioxide insufflation helps open up the dissection planes, guiding the procedure towards the lower border of the pancreas for ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies two avascular planes and works on the correct plane, ensuring the fat moves up with the specimen. The pulsation of the superior rectal artery is visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects close to the vascular sheath, pushing the vessel upwards. The dissection occurs just below the vascular sheath, with attention to identifying the left ureter before proceeding near the inferior mesenteric artery and ligating it.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and ensures it is well away from the field of dissection. The dissection progresses in the TME plane, between the peritoneum of the viscera and the presacral fascia, avoiding the deeper plane where pre-sacral vessels run. The incision is carefully made to avoid proximity to the ureter, as the dissection moves inferiorly and cranially towards the take-off of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to assist in dissection, carefully identifying and preserving the thin fascia covering the ureter. The dissection progresses inferiorly, ensuring the ureter and gonadal vessels remain protected by fascia. Upon encountering a vessel, the surgeon adjusts the dissection plane to avoid exposing the ureter, confirming the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to take down small vessels and nerve branches posterior to the vascular sheath pedicle. The root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is exposed, and a vessel sealer is used to manage lymphatic channels, minimizing postoperative lymphorrhea. Care is taken to avoid injuring the hypogastric nerves by not dissecting too far inferiorly. Lymphovascular structures from the base of the IMA and over the aorta are carefully removed.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and seal tissues around the small branches leading to the mesocolon of the descending colon. A right angle or Maryland dissector is employed to maneuver around the vessel, which is clearly visible. The vessel is secured with a clip, and the surgeon may complement this with a sealer or cold scissors depending on the available pedicle length.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the fascia covering the ureter, with the gonadal vein visible as a bluish tubular structure. The dissection progresses laterally, revealing the psoas muscle and the avascular plane. The peritoneum is incised along the line of Toldt, approaching the lower border of the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein is visible, and the surgeon works near the duodenojejunal flexure, carefully managing peritoneal attachments and avoiding deep dissection to prevent nerve injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects a thin peritoneal layer, lifting the inferior mesenteric vein to develop an avascular plane inferior to the inferior mesenteric artery. Using blunt suction, the peritoneum covering Gerota's fascia is meticulously separated from the mesocolon, revealing the pancreas. The dissection progresses towards the left shoulder until the spleen is visible, maintaining precision to avoid staining tissues.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure from the medial side, using the medial to lateral technique. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated and ligated at the lower border of the pancreas, followed by division with a sealer. The anterior surface of the pancreas becomes visible as the mesentery attachments are released, allowing for a clearer view of the pancreas during the dissection.

The surgeon tunnels below the mesentery on top of the pancreas, entering the lesser sac. The stomach and transverse colon are visible, along with vessels in the gastrocolic omentum. The dissection progresses laterally towards the spleen, with the gonadal vessels visible. The fascia covering the gonadal vessels is preserved, and the plane is opened using carbon dioxide. The surgeon increases the incision on the peritoneum to release the colon, tackling small attachments with blunt and sharp dissection. The medial to lateral mobilization of the splenic flexure is performed, using a monopolar device to advance towards the splenic flexure area.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to open up dissection planes, releasing thin fascial attachments around the descending colon. The colon is visibly free due to prior medial and inferior dissection. The surgeon employs a monopolar hook to release flimsy fibrous tissues, progressing towards the splenic flexure area. The dissection follows avascular embryological planes, minimizing the risk of encountering major vascular structures.

The surgeon releases a small fibrous strand, revealing a gauze placed as a marker on the medial side. Thin attachments are dissected to mobilize the colon from the splenic flexure area. The spleen becomes visible as the dissection progresses, with the surgeon using electrocautery to separate remaining fibrous tissues. The omental attachments towards the splenic flexure and distal transverse mesocolon are released to ensure tension-free mobilization. The lateral attachments are fully released, and further dissection of the gastrocolic omentum is performed to free the mesocolon.

The surgeon extends the incision on the peritoneum cranially and caudally, progressing the dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane below the sacral promontory. The dissection stays close to the mesocolon and mesorectum, avoiding the presacral fascia, revealing the left hypogastric nerve and ureter, both clearly visible and preserved. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection with cautery to precisely separate the thin fascial plane covering the nerve.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to open up the dissection planes near the pelvic sidewall, ensuring careful identification of the correct anatomical planes to avoid injury to the internal iliac vein and ureter. Carbon dioxide insufflation aids in visualizing the dissection path, allowing for precise navigation and preservation of critical structures.

The surgeon maintains close proximity to the mesorectum, avoiding excessive inferior dissection. The fascia is observed to fall and fuse with the pelvic sidewall or sacrum. The planes become well-defined, allowing for smooth progression. The surgeon incises slightly adherent tissues at precise levels to avoid injury, following the thin fascia. A posterior dissection is performed before moving to the lateral aspects, ensuring the correct planes are identified and adhered to.

The surgeon continues the total mesorectal excision (TME), with clear visualization of the mesorectum and prominent vessels. The dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor, with carbon dioxide aiding in opening the planes. The surgeon identifies and preserves nerves near the dissection area, while managing fumes that occasionally obscure vision. A large vessel, likely the median sacral vessel, is visible in the midline. The dissection extends to the lateral and anterior aspects, with traction and counter traction facilitating the opening of planes. Small nerve branches and the middle rectal artery are identified, and a vessel sealer is used for dissection in the middle rectal area.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to guide dissection near the right seminal vesicle, preserving fascia and fat for nerve protection. Short bursts of cautery coagulate small vessels. The prostate becomes visible as dissection progresses below its level, aiming to include the Denonvilliers fascia with the specimen for additional anterior margin. The surgeon moves to the left side, revealing the pelvic sidewall and levator muscles, and continues dissection superiorly using monopolar cautery to incise fused peritoneum in the midline.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to release the peritoneum, revealing the prostate more clearly. Dissection continues on the left side, distinguishing between mesorectal fat and fat surrounding the seminal vesicle. Traction and counter-traction are applied to maintain the avascular plane, minimizing bleeding. The surgeon works close to the left seminal vesicle, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection. The pelvic sidewall and twitching levator muscles are visible, with facial strands between the pelvic sidewall and rectum clearly dissected. The dissection aims for a cylindrical approach, releasing attachments for comprehensive exposure.

The surgeon dissects further on the left side, revealing the shape of the prostate in the surgical field. Short bursts of cautery are used to join the left and right dissection spaces, maintaining traction and counter traction to clearly define the thin fascial planes. The prostate is visible, and a small bleeder on its surface is coagulated. The dissection progresses towards the inferior portion of the bladder, with the denonvillia fascia becoming clearly visible.

The surgeon is dissecting above the Denonvilliers fascia, which appears as a taut structure, using gentle traction and cautery to avoid bleeding from small venules. The prostate is visible and appears bare, with small vessels running across it. The surgeon identifies and seals branches of the middle rectal artery supplying the rectum, while managing tissue fluid and lymphatic drainage with gauze.

The surgeon continues dissection on the right side, addressing small oozes and blood vessels meticulously. The pelvic side wall becomes visible, similar to the left side, as the dissection progresses to isolate the rectum.

The surgeon works close to the prostate, pushing down the thick white Denonvillier's fascia. As they move caudally, the fascia is incised to transition to the normal plane, avoiding nerve and urethra injury. Monopolar cautery is used to incise the fascia, revealing the mesorectum plane below. Thin fibrous structures are visible, and hemostasis is maintained despite deeper dissection. The assisting hand uses suction to manage fumes in the narrow area.

The surgeon uses cautery to incise the denonvillier fascia on the anterior aspect, extending the incision laterally. The edge of the incised fascia is visible, with the continuation of the fascia on the lateral aspect. Suction is used to clear fumes, improving visibility. The surgeon carefully progresses with the dissection, ensuring not to extend beyond the seminal vesicles to avoid nerve injury. The final portion of the denonvillier fascia is divided, revealing the fat surrounding the mesorectum, allowing for rapid progression in the dissection plane.

The surgeon uses a short burst of cautery to dissect the last portion of the Denonvilliers fascia, which is adherent around the rectum. The dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor, with the rectum being isolated at this level.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) by releasing the remaining strands of the Denonvilliers fascia at the pelvic floor. Traction and counter-traction techniques are used to open up the dissection planes. Monopolar cautery is applied to manage small oozes, with bipolar cautery available for more significant bleeding. The dissection proceeds below the Denonvilliers fascia, following an avascular plane for a comprehensive cylindrical dissection.

The surgeon isolates the rectum by removing surrounding fat, preparing it for division using a stapler. A digital rectal examination confirms the tumor is not palpable, indicating successful dissection beyond the tumor site. The rectum is divided using a 45mm green roticulating stapler. The specimen is extracted, and the inferior mesenteric pedicle is ligated. The colon is incised, and a purse string suture is placed to secure the anvil for an end-to-end anastomosis. The stapler is used to complete the anastomosis, ensuring no twisting of the bowel and proper alignment.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and insufflates air with saline in the pelvis to check for any leakage.
